key_points: Landscape is continuously worn away by two processes: Weathering (breaking of rocks) and Erosion (wearing away by water, wind, and ice).
A river creates distinct landforms as it flows, such as waterfalls, meanders, ox-bow lakes, and floodplains.
Sea waves contribute to coastal landforms through both erosional and depositional activities.
Glaciers, often called 'rivers of ice', erode the landscape by bulldozing soil and stones to expose the solid rock below.
Wind is the primary agent of erosion and deposition in deserts, creating unique sandy structures.
examples: River Landforms: Meanders (large curves), Ox-bow lakes (cutoff loops), and Deltas (mouth of the river).
Sea Landforms: Sea Caves, Sea Arches, Stacks (broken walls), and Beaches (deposits).
Ice Landforms: Deep hollows (which become lakes) and Glacial Moraines (deposited material).
Desert Landforms: Mushroom rocks (narrow base, wide top), Sand dunes, and Loess (fine dust deposits).
formulas: Work of River = Erosion (upper course) + Deposition (lower course)
exam_tips: Note that the highest waterfall is Angel Falls of Venezuela in South America.
Remember that an ox-bow lake is formed when a meander loop cuts off from the main river.
Understand that 'Mushroom rocks' get their shape because wind erodes the lower part of the rock more than the upper part.
Identify that 'Loess' is found extensively in China.
